justification: All pass criteria met: (1) 4 bounded contexts identified and named (Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification). (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly identified — escalations call out multi-driver zone behavior, and the domain_analysis notes contexts should communicate via domain events. BR-9 explicitly flags that recalculation must preserve the vehicle class constraint across context boundaries. (3) Vehicle class rule (BR-2) correctly marked invariant: true; stop duration (BR-7) correctly marked invariant: false; notification window (BR-6) correctly marked invariant: false. (4) 11 acceptance criteria provided, far exceeding the minimum of 4. (5) All three recalculation triggers enumerated (new delivery added, delivery failed, driver deviation) in BR-3/BR-4/BR-5.

observations.text: Excellent bounded context separation with 4 clearly named contexts and explicit note that they should communicate via domain events, not direct coupling
11 well-structured Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy paths, edge cases, and multi-driver scenarios

Strong invariant classification — vehicle class (BR-2) and zone uniqueness (BR-8) correctly marked as true invariants; configurable policies (stop duration, notification window, recalculation triggers) correctly marked as false
BR-9 is a sophisticated addition — explicitly calling out that recalculation must preserve vehicle class constraints, identifying this as a cross-context integration point
10 edge cases identified with expected behaviors, many calling out spec gaps that need product clarification
Escalations are well-structured with options and recommendations, particularly the multi-driver zone assignment and notification retraction issues

Route recalculation triggers (BR-3, BR-4, BR-5) are marked invariant: false, but the rubric expected invariant: true since the system must always recalculate on these events. Bird's justification is reasonable (batching/deferring is valid) but diverges from the expected classification

Cross-context dependency between route recalculation and delivery lifecycle is not called out as explicitly as expected — the rubric wanted a specific statement about shared data contracts between these contexts. The output implies it via domain events but doesn't name the specific dependency formally
Rich ubiquitous language section with 12 well-defined terms grounding the domain model
Business impact analysis covers financial, operational, user, and risk dimensions with specific stakeholder mapping — goes beyond what was required

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 4 bounded contexts identified (Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification). Cross-context dependencies surfaced in escalations (multi-driver zone coordination, recalculation triggers spanning Delivery→Route contexts) and edge cases. Vehicle class constraint correctly marked invariant:true (BR-1), stop duration correctly marked invariant:false (BR-8), notification window invariant:false (BR-7). 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided. Route recalculation triggers enumerated across BR-4/5/6.

observations.text: Excellent bounded context decomposition with 4 clearly named contexts and well-defined ownership boundaries
10 comprehensive acceptance criteria covering happy paths, edge cases (duplicate notification prevention), and configuration scenarios
Invariant classification is precise — vehicle class and zone constraints correctly marked true, all configurable policies correctly marked false
Rich edge case analysis (10 scenarios) covering race conditions, notification retraction, mid-shift vehicle changes, and zone boundary changes
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations, particularly the multi-driver coordination ambiguity
Cross-context dependencies could be more explicitly called out as formal integration points within the domain_analysis section itself, rather than being distributed across escalations and edge cases

Route recalculation triggers (BR-4/5/6) are all marked invariant:false, which is defensible but the rubric expected invariant:true — Bird's justification is reasonable (recalculation is optimization policy, not data integrity) but diverges from expected classification
BR-3 (auto-route on shift start) correctly identified as policy rather than invariant, showing nuanced domain reasoning
Confidence level of 55 is appropriately calibrated given the significant spec gaps identified

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 4 bounded contexts identified (Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification). Cross-context dependencies explicitly surfaced in escalations and edge cases (e.g., route recalculation depending on delivery lifecycle state changes, vehicle class as a Fleet Management constraint enforced in Route Planning). Vehicle class rule correctly marked invariant: true (BR-1), stop duration correctly marked invariant: false (BR-7), notification window correctly marked invariant: false (BR-6). 9 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well exceeding the 4 minimum. All three route recalculation triggers (new delivery, failed delivery, driver deviation) correctly enumerated as BR-3, BR-4, BR-5.

observations.text: Exceptional ubiquitous language section with 10 precisely defined terms, each with operational implications noted
9 business rules with well-reasoned invariant classifications and testable assertions — goes well beyond the minimum
9 acceptance criteria covering happy paths, edge cases (idempotent notifications, incompatible vehicle class during recalc), and configuration overrides
9 edge cases identified including concurrency (simultaneous triggers), empty states (zero deliveries), and the subtle geographic vs. sequence deviation ambiguity

Cross-context dependencies explicitly escalated — vehicle class compatibility model, recalculation triggers spanning Delivery and Route contexts, shift-end handoff between Driver and Delivery contexts
4 well-structured escalations with options and recommendations, directly surfacing integration points for MJ
BR-2 (shift-start route generation) correctly classified as invariant: false with sound reasoning that the trigger is a business policy, not a physical constraint

The bounded_context field uses a flat string format ('Primary: Route Planning. Adjacent: ...') rather than a structured breakdown — the richer context analysis is embedded in business_context prose instead

Could have been more explicit about the data contract between Delivery Lifecycle and Route Planning contexts — the cross-context dependency is surfaced in escalations but not formally modeled as an integration point
